learning_outcomes.description: Conduct a critical review of relevant academic literature and/or architectural projects and proposals in response to a culture and place of indigeneous people.
Apply an appropriate strategy to undertaking research informed by cultural awareness.
Evaluate primary and secondary research evidence related to indigenous perspectives.
Independently and/or collaboratively formulate a research proposition that is grounded in indigenous cultures.
Independently prepare a research synthesis report/essay.

assessments.assessment_title: REMOTE PLACES AND SOCIAL HOUSING CULTURES RESEARCH JOURNAL
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale: 

This individual task requires students to develop their own critical reflections on lectures and tutorial learning task documenting and collating these into a research journal surveying research literature and other scholarly information each week. The reflective journal should cover informed understanding of core topics related Indigenous housing and cultures, place, place settings, well-being, energy and climate. Students will form in class small research groups (3-4 persons) to evaluate literature and critically discuss Indigenous social housing research covering national, state and different regional place settings in Australia. Students will individually synthesise their own critical summaries comparing evidence-based information from Australia, Queensland and Northern Territory on the following topics:

  • Dimensions of Indigenous social housing, climate and wellbeing
  • Australian, regional and remote geographies, place and climatic zones
  • Either Regional townships of Gununa, (Qld) or Jurnkkurakurr/Tennant Creek, (NT) or Yarrabah (Qld) infrastructure settings
  • Indigenous housing cultures, supply, needs and aspirations
  • Culturally responsive housing design
  • National, State and regional housing and population statistics

Complex/Authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) successful completion of assessment in this Course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Ai and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised.

assessments.assessment_title: REMOTE SOCIAL HOUSING AND CLIMATE/ENERGY/WELLBEING POSTER
assessments.due_date:
Presentation of draft poster in class 21/09/2026 9:00 am

Final Poster Submission 25/09/2026 2:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale:

The purpose of this two-part assessment is to demonstrate and communicate your research intention on remote social housing that will inform your final assessment for this course. Students will synthesise research focus on social housing and either climate or energy or wellbeing in the form of a poster. The format of the poster allows for a vibrant engaging communication of your research proposition combining text, graphics and references. Each student will incorporate two study regions identifying comparative research question/s and key supporting evidence to enhance climate resilience and liveability. Your research intention should propose a core research question, but is not expected be answered in this submission because it will be the subject of further study in the final assessment. The submission is individual work comprised of (a) graphic and written submission and (b) in class draft presentation (oral and visual).

Complex/Authentic assessment using AI or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) successful completion of assessment in this Course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Ai and MT tools.

Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.

assessments.assessment_title: SOCIAL HOUSING CLIMATE RESILIENCE AND LIVEABILITY ENABLING STUDY
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Project, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale: 

Undertake the design of individual social housing and climate resilience study defining evidence-informed methods to enhance liveability and wellbeing at a specific place. The final study scope and research question/s identified in Assessment 2 will be refined in your study using a combination of anonymised survey data, relevant literature and graphics. Note anonymised housing survey data will be provided by the Course Co-ordinator.

The submission will present a critical reflection of the design of your evidence-informed method, identifying critical problems encountered in designing your study. It should demonstrate an informed understanding of Indigenous social housing and place combined with either wellbeing or climate resilience. You are required to include targeted graphic illustrations (e.g. climate modelling showing the attributes of the climate region) and population profile tables of a specific place/s. You should identify any potential constraints and weaknesses in your study method and relevance of anonymised survey data to your study. Your submission should not exceed 2,000 words (excluding annotated bibliography) describing your research method and critical reflection. You must include all Ai logged prompts, responses and refinements used in the development of your research method, understanding of the survey data or graphics that have assist you in your critical thematic analysis and graphics used to communicate your method.

Complex/Authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) successful completion of assessment in this Course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Ai and MT tools.
